  [Publishing Unified-Model Policies to Exchange](policies-pdk-publish-policies).

- `make help`: Lists all the `make` commands provided by PDK.

## See Also

- [PDK Troubleshooting](policies-pdk-troubleshooting)
---

# Defining a Policy Schema Definition

> For the full documentation index, see: https://docs.mulesoft.com/llms.txt

Similar to the policies listed in the
[Inbound Policies Directory](../../gateway/latest/policies-included-directory), custom policies have
configuration parameters. Use the configuration parameters to improve the usability of your custom
policy and support a wider range of API configurations.

To define custom configuration parameters, edit the `definition/gcl.yaml` file. The `gcl.yaml` file
is written in the Gateway Configuration Language (GCL). However, you do not need prior GCL knowledge
to use this tutorial.

The configuration parameters you define in the `gcl.yaml` appear as variables in the
`src/generated/config.rs` file after you run the `make build-asset-file` command. You set any user
input you need for implementing the Rust source code in the `gcl.yaml` file.

Configuration parameters appear in the API Manager UI when applying a policy to Omni Gateway running
in Connected Mode. When applying the policy to Omni Gateway running in Local Mode, it is your
responsibility to distribute a configuration `yaml` file for your policy.

The default `gcl.yaml` file has the following content:

```yaml
apiVersion: gateway.mulesoft.com.v1alpha1
kind: Extension
metadata:
  labels:
    title: my-custom-policy
    category: Custom
spec:
  extends:
    - name: extension-definition
  properties:
    stringProperty:
    type: string
```

The `gcl.yaml` file contains two sections:

- `metadata`: Defines information about the policy
- `properties`: Defines the policyâs configuration parameters

To define a schema definition:

1.  [Define Metadata](#define-metadata)
2.  [Define Parameters](#define-parameters)
3.  [Use Custom Parameters in Rust Source Code](#use-custom-parameters-in-rust-source-code)

## Define Metadata

Add labels in the metadata section to enrich the data shown in the UI when applying the policy.

To define the metadata, edit the `metadata` in the `gcl.yaml` file.

The supported labels are:

- `title`: The policy name that appears in the UI.
- `description`: A brief explanation of the policyâs behavior.
- `category`: The category the of the policy. The possible categories are `security`, `compliance`,
  `transformation`, `quality of service`, and `troubleshooting`.

For example:

```yaml
metadata:
  labels:
    title: JSON Threat Protection
    description: Protects against malicious JSON in API requests.
    category: Security
```

`labels` are suggested but not required.

## Define Outbound Policies

By default, PDK creates policies as inbound policies. To create an outbound policy, edit the
`labels` section in the `gcl.yaml` file. Add the `metadata/capabilities/injectionPoint: outbound`
label, for example:

```yaml
metadata:
  labels:
    title: My Outbound Policy
    description: An outbound security policy
    category: Security
    metadata/capabilities/injectionPoint: outbound
```

## Define Parameters

To add parameters, edit the `spec.properties` section in the `gcl.yaml` file.

For example:

```yaml
properties:
  username:
    type: string
  password:
    type: string
```

After defining your properties, you can:

- [Define Required Properties and Default Values](#define-required-properties-and-default-values)
- [Define Supported Types](#define-supported-types)

### Define Required Properties and Default Values

You can define each property listed in the `gcl.yaml` file as `required` and set a `default` value
for the property.

To define properties as required, list them in the `spec.required` object. For example, the
following policy requires a `password` but not a `username`:

```yaml
properties:
  username:
    type: string
  password:
    type: string
required:
  - password
```

In the above configuration example, applying a policy without a password results in a failed
configuration.

The following example defines a default value for each property:

```yaml
properties:
  username:
    type: string
    default: user1
  password:
    type: string
    default: 12345678
required:
  - password
```

If you do not set a value for a required property when applying a policy, the property uses the
default value.

For non-required properties, the API Manager UI suggests the configured default value for
properties. If you do not specify the value for non-required properties, the property is not
configured.

In the following configuration table, if the policy was applied in API Manager, `user2` would appear
as a suggested value for `username` but is not configured if the value is omitted:

<table><colgroup><col> <col> <col></colgroup><tbody><tr><td><p>Policy Configuration</p></td><td><p>Effective Configuration</p></td><td><p>Configuration Status</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><code>{}</code></p></td><td><p><code>{"password": "12345678"}</code></p></td><td><p>Success</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><code>{"username": "user2"}</code></p></td><td><p><code>{"password": "12345678",<br>"username": "user2"}</code></p></td><td><p>Success</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><code>{"password": "password"}</code></p></td><td><p><code>{"password": "password"}</code></p></td><td><p>Success</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><code>{"password": "password",<br>"username": "user2"}</code></p></td><td><p><code>{"password": "password",<br>"username": "user2"}</code></p></td><td><p>Success</p></td></tr></tbody></table>

### Define Sensitive Data

Protect sensitive data, such as passwords or certificates, by adding the `security:sensitive`
characteristic:

```yaml
properties:
    key:
        type: string
        default: "YourKey"
        "@context": {
        "@characteristics": [
          "security:sensitive"
        ]
      }
```

When applying the policy, the input value to the sensitive parameter is not visible to the user:

![The character inputs to the key parameter appear as black dots.](_images/sensitive-data.png)

Toggling **Show** reveals any new data currently being entered. However, you cannot reveal
previously stored data. For example, if you configure the example **Key** parameter and apply the
policy, you cannot view the parameter if you edit the policyâs configuration in the future.

### Define Conditional Parameters

Render policy configuration parameters dynamically by establishing conditions to make fields
visible. In the following code example, the `encryptionKey` parameter appears if the user selects
`true` for the `encryptBody` parameter:

```yaml
properties:
  encryptBody:
    type: boolean
    default: false
  encryptionKey:
    type: string
    "@rendering":
      "@visibleOn":
        - property: encryptBody
          value: true
```

In the following code example, the `encryptionKey` parameter is initially visible and disappears if
the user selects `false` for the for the `encryptBody` parameter:

```yaml
properties:
  encryptBody:
    type: boolean
    default: true
  encryptionKey:
    type: string
    "@rendering":
      "@visibleOn":
        - property: encryptBody
          value: true
```

Conditional parameters have the following restrictions:

- You can only define a single condition per parameter.
- Only `string`, `boolean`, or `integer` parameters are supported as the condition parameter.
- The conditionally rendered parameter cannot be an `object` or `enum`.
- Required properties that are conditionally rendered must have a default value defined. In the
  above examples, if `encryptionKey` is required, it requires a default value.
- Only root-level parameters support conditional rendering. Inner properties of arrays or objects do
  not support conditional rendering.

### Define Enumerated Parameters

Limit userâs input values by defining parameters with the `enum` property, for example:

```yaml
properties:
  signingMethod:
    type: string
    enum:
      - rsa
      - hmac
```

In the API Manager UI, `enum` parameters render as radio buttons for three or fewer enumerated
values and as a dropdown field for more. For Omni Gateway running in Local Mode, the user must
configure the parameter as one of the enumerated values or the policy configuration fails.

Enumerated parameters donât support the `@visibleOn` tag. To learn more about how to render
variables dynamically, see [Define Conditional Parameters](#define-conditional-parameters).

### Define Supported Types

Define a supported data type for each policy parameter to ensure the policy receives the correct
data.

The supported types are:

String

The string type specifies that the data is text.

```yaml
properties:
  password:
    type: string
```

The `string` type also supports enables you to configure a `format` type.

The supported formatting types are:

- `dataweave`: Specifies the input parameter is a DataWeave variable and notifies Omni Gateway to
  transform the input. For more information about DataWeave expressions, see
  [Define DataWeave Expressions](#dataweave-expressions).
- `service`: Specifies the input parameter is a URI and automatically creates an Omni Gateway
  service to enable HTTP calls from the policy.
- `ipRange`: Specifies the expected text is a range of IPs and provides additional validation on the
  input.
- `uri`: Specifies the expected text is a URI and provides additional validation on the input. Only
  use `uri` when the URI is not a service making HTTP calls from the policy.

  The following yaml snippet demonstrates all string formats:

  ```yaml
  properties:
    externalService:
      type: string
      format: service
      default: "https://auth-service:9000/login"
    token:
      type: string
      format: dataweave
      default: "#[splitBy(attributes.headers['Authorization'], ' ')[1]]"
    blockedIps:
      type: string
      format: ipRange
      default: "192.168.3.1/30"
    loginUrl:
      type: string
      format: uri
      default: "https://anypoint.mulesoft.com/login"
  ```

Number

The `number` type specifies data as a floating point value.

For example:

```yaml
properties:
  squareRoot:
    type: number
    default: 1.414213562
  seconds:
    type: number
    default: 10
```

Integer

The `integer` type specifies data as an integer value.

For example:

```yaml
properties:
  maxHeaders:
    type: integer
    default: 10
```

Boolean

The `boolean` type specifies data as a boolean value. Supported values are `true` or `false`. For
example:

```yaml
properties:
  skipValidation:
    type: boolean
    default: false
  requireCredentials:
    type: boolean
    default: true
```

Array

The `array` type specifies the data as a list of ordered elements. For the `array` type, you must
also specify `items.type` to specify the data type of the items in the array.

For example:

```yaml
properties:
  scopes:
    type: array
    items:
      type: string
    default: ["READ", "WRITE"]
```

Object

The `object` type specifies the data as a list of ordered elements with multiple properties. For the
`object` type, you must also specify `properties.type` for each element in the object.

For example:

```yaml
properties:
  user:
    type: object
    properties:
      id:
        type: string
      name:
        type: string
    required:
      - id
      - name
    default: { "id": "1", "name": "John Doe" }
```

### Define DataWeave Expressions

The `dataweave` format for `string` type parameters specifies the input parameter as a DataWeave
expression.

The `dataweave` variable supports the following expression bindings:

```yaml
<configuration-parameter>:
  type: string
  format: dataweave
  bindings:
    payload:
      mimeType: text
    attributes: true
    authentication: false
    vars: []
```

- `payload`: The body of the incoming request. Define the `mimeType` as either `text`, `json`, or
  `xml` to set how to read the payload. By default, `mimeType` is set to `text`. If the
  `content-type` header is bound to the evaluator, then the payload is interpreted according to the
  value of the `content-type` header. To restrict the use of the `payload` variable, exclude the
  payload variable as shown in this example:

  ```yaml
  bindings:
    attributes: true
    authentication: false
    vars: []
  ```

- `attributes`: Attributes, such as headers, of the incoming request. Set `attributes` to `true` to
  enable the policy to read the attributes of the incoming request. By default, `attributes` is set
  to `true`.
- `authentication`: Authentication information of the incoming request. Set `authentication` to
  `true` to enable the policy to read authentication details of the incoming request. By default,
  `authentication` is set to `false`.
- `vars`: Specifies the names of the variables that are available for the policy to use. By default,
  `vars` is empty. When the policy is applied, if the user inputs a variable that is not defined in
  the schema, the policy evaluates the variable as `null`.

For more information about the supported expression bindings, see
[Predefined Variables](../../dataweave/latest/dataweave-variables-context).

If the userâs expression contains bindings that are not defined in the policyâs schema when applying
the policy, the policy configuration fails. For example, if the user expression is
`[authentication.principal]` and `authentication` is not set to `true`, an error similar to the
following occurs:

test-local-flex-1 | \[flex-gateway-envoy\]\[error\] wasm log main: \[policy:
ingress-http-jwt-auth-v1-0-impl-1.default\]\[api: ingress-http.default.svc\] Launcher problem:
Failed to parse configuration '{"dw":"P\[\[\\".\\", \\"0-24\\", \[\\":ref\\", \\"0-14\\",
\\"authentication\\"\], \[\\":str\\", \\"15-24\\", \\"principal\\"\]\],
\\"#\[authentication.principal\]\\"\]"}'. Cause: Error compiling expression: Unknown symbol
\`authentication\` at line 1 column 141

When evaluating the DataWeave expression in your policy, you must provide the script to resolve the
expression. To learn how to implement DataWeave expressions in your Rust source code, see
[Implement DataWeave Expressions](policies-pdk-configure-features#use-dataweave-expressions).

For more information about DataWeave support for Omni Gateway, see
[DataWeave Support in Omni Gateway Policies](../../gateway/latest/policies-flex-dataweave-support).

## Use Custom Parameters in Rust Source Code

After you modify the `gcl.yaml` file, run one of the following commands to propagate the changes to
the `src/generated/config.rs` rust file and the JSON schema for the API Manager UI:

- `make build-asset-files` only propagates the changes to `src/generated/config.rs`.

  ```cmd
  make build-asset-files
  ```

- `make build` propagates the changes to `src/generated/config.rs` and generates the policyâs target
  files. Do not run `make build` if you do not want to override your policyâs target file:

  ```cmd
  make build
  ```

  For more information about compiling your policy, see
  [Compiling Custom Policies](policies-pdk-compile-policies).

Properties defined in the `gcl.yaml` files are mapped to the Rust data types in the `config.rs` Rust
file as follows:

<table><colgroup><col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th>gcl.yaml</th><th>config.rs</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p><code>string</code></p></td><td><p><code>String</code></p></td></tr><tr><td><p><code>uri</code> string</p></td><td><p><code>String</code></p></td></tr><tr><td><p><code>ipRange</code> string</p></td><td><p><code>String</code></p></td></tr><tr><td><p><code>dataweave</code> string</p></td><td><p><code>pdk::script::Script</code></p></td></tr><tr><td><p><code>number</code></p></td><td><p><code>f64</code></p></td></tr><tr><td><p><code>integer</code></p></td><td><p><code>i64</code></p></td></tr><tr><td><p><code>boolean</code></p></td><td><p><code>bool</code></p></td></tr><tr><td><p><code>array</code></p></td><td><p><code>vec</code> containing the specified item type</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><code>object</code></p></td><td><p><code>struct</code> containing the specified property types</p></td></tr></tbody></table>

> [!IMPORTANT] If a parameter in `gcl.yaml` is not defined as ârequiredâ, the parameterâs
> counterpart in the `config.rs` is wrapped in an âOptionâ object.
>
> Validate that the parameter is present before unwrapping. Unwrapping a parameter that is not
> present may cause a `panic!`.
>
> Use one of the following validation examples to ensure the parameter is present:
>
> - `if let Some(val) = myValue`
> - `if myValue.is_some() {myValue.unwrap()}`

For example, the following `gcl.yaml` files produces the following `struct` in `config.rs`:

1.  `gcl.yaml`:

    ```yaml
    properties:
        tokenExtractor:
          type: string
          format: dataweave
          default: "#[dw::core::Strings::substringAfter(attributes.headers['Authorization'], 'Bearer ')]"
        reject_missing_tokens:
          type: boolean
        max_chars:
          type: number
      required:
        - tokenExtractor
    ```

2.  `config.rs`:

    ```rust
    pub struct Config {
        #[serde(alias = "max_chars")]
        pub max_chars: Option<f64>,
        #[serde(alias = "reject_missing_tokens")]
        pub reject_missing_tokens: Option<bool>,
        #[serde(alias = "tokenExtractor")]
        pub token_extractor: pdk::script::Script,
    }
    ```

For example, you can access the values in `struct Config` with the following `.operators` in the
`entrypoint` function:

```rust
#[entrypoint]
async fn configure(launcher: Launcher, Configuration(configuration): Configuration,) -> Result<(), LaunchError> {
    let config: Config = serde_json::from_slice(&configuration)?;
    match config.reject_missing_tokens {
```

## See Also

- [PDK Troubleshooting](policies-pdk-troubleshooting)
---

# Debugging Deployed Custom Policies

> For the full documentation index, see: https://docs.mulesoft.com/llms.txt

Errors that arenât present in the PDKâs debugging and testing environments might be present when the
policy is [applied to an API instance](policies-pdk-apply-policies) in a production environment.

To use debug logs in a production environment, see
[Troubleshooting Self-Managed Gateway with Debug Logs](../../gateway/latest/flex-troubleshoot-debug-logs).

For more information about monitoring Omni Gateway, see
[Monitoring Omni Gateway](../../gateway/latest/flex-gateway-monitor).

## See Also

- [Debugging Custom Policies with the PDK Debugging Playground](policies-pdk-debug-local)
- [Writing Integration Tests](policies-pdk-integration-tests)
---

# Debugging Custom Policies with the PDK Debugging Playground

> For the full documentation index, see: https://docs.mulesoft.com/llms.txt

To test the functionality of your policy, Omni Gateway Policy Development Kit (PDK) provides a
pre-configured API instance located in the `<root-directory>/playground/config` directory.

The PDK Debugging Playground only supports Omni Gateway running in Local Mode in a Docker container.
If you plan to deploy or policy to Omni Gateway running in Connected Mode or to a different
platform, you should still first test your policy in PDK by using Omni Gateway running in Local Mode
in a Docker container.

> [!IMPORTANT] You can run Omni Gateway in Local Mode on Docker in Windows only to develop and test
> your policies. However, Omni Gateway does not support Windows in production environments.

To test your policy, complete the following tasks:

1.  [Before You Begin](#before-you-begin)
2.  [Register an Omni Gateway Instance in Local Mode](#register)
3.  [Configure the Policy](#configure-the-policy)
4.  [Add Additional Policies](#add-additional-policies) if necessary
5.  [Deploy the Policy with PDK](#deploy-the-policy-with-pdk)

## Before You Begin

Ensure that you have:

- [Installed Docker](https://www.docker.com/).
- [Compiled your custom policy project](policies-pdk-compile-policies).

## Register an Omni Gateway Instance in Local Mode

To begin using the `make run` command to debug your custom policy, register an Omni Gateway in Local
Mode.

For the `run` command to work, an Omni Gateway `registration.yaml` file must exist in the
`<root-directory>/playground/config` directory. Create this file by running the registration command
in the directory, or move a `registration.yaml` file of a previously registered Omni Gateway to the
directory.

The `make run` command only supports Omni Gateways running in Local Mode in a Docker Container. To
register Omni Gateway, see:

- [Register and Run with a Username and Password in a Docker Container](../../gateway/latest/flex-local-reg-run-up#docker)
- [Register and Run with a Connected App in a Docker Container](../../gateway/latest/flex-local-reg-run-app#docker)
- [Register and Run with a Token in a Docker Container](../../gateway/latest/flex-local-reg-run-token#docker)

> [!IMPORTANT] Run only the registration command. Do not run the Docker start command because the
> `make run` command completes this step.

Create a different registration file on each device testing the policy.

The registration file is ignored for storage in remote repositories and is listed in the
`.gitignore` file located at the root of the project directory.

## Configure the Policy

Depending on the configurations listed in the `definition/gcl.yaml` file you configured in
[Defining a Policy Schema Definition](policies-pdk-create-schema-definition), you may have to
configure some parameters before running the policy locally.

For example, if the `gcl.yaml` file is as follows:

```yaml
apiVersion: gateway.mulesoft.com.v1alpha1
kind: Extension
metadata:
  labels:
    title: my-custom-policy
    category: Security
spec:
  extends:
    - name: extension-definition
  properties:
    user:
      type: string
      default: user1
    password:
      type: string
    description:
      type: string
  required:
    - user
    - password
```

Update the `/playground/config/api.yaml` file with your configuration value:

```yaml
apiVersion: gateway.mulesoft.com/v1alpha1
kind: ApiInstance
metadata:
  name: ingress-http
spec:
  address: http://0.0.0.0:8081
  services:
    upstream:
      address: http://backend
      routes:
        - config:
            destinationPath: /anything/echo/
  policies:
    - policyRef:
        name: my-custom-policy
      config:
        password: 12345678
```

In this example, the `user` is set to the default value `user1`, the `password` is set to
`12345678`, and no description is provided.

## Add Additional Policies

You can also apply additional policies to the test API instance to test your custom policies
behavior alongside other policies.

However, your custom policy under development must be the first policy specified, for example:

```yaml
policies:
  - policyRef:
      name: my-custom-policy
    config:
      password: 12345678
  - policyRef:
      name: additional-policy
    config:
```

## Deploy the Policy with PDK

After you complete the previous steps, run the `make run` command from the policyâs root directory
to deploy policy:

```cmd
make run
```

This command starts the two Docker containers in the
`<root-directory>/playground/docker-compose.yaml` file:

- `Local-flex`: The Omni Gateway instance Docker container that executes the custom policy. This
  Omni Gateway listens for requests made to `localhost:8081`.
- `Backend`: The Docker Container that runs the backend service for the Omni Gateway instance. This
  backend service echoes any request it receives.

> [!NOTE] To stop the Docker containers, press `Cmd+c` or `Ctrl+c` depending on your device from the
> terminal running the containers.

Once the containers are running, you can make requests to the API instance, for example:

```cmd
curl --location --request POST 'http://0.0.0.0:8081/some/route' \
--header 'Token: mytoken'
```

The backend service returns an echo response including the requestsâs route, body, and headers sent
with any modification performed by the custom policy or additional policies. Any logs included in
the custom policyâs source code are visible in the terminal running the containers.

## Configure the Loglevel

By default, PDKâs debugging environment enables logs with the `debug` `loglevel` configured.

To change the `loglevel`, edit the `logging.runtimeLogs.logLevel` value in
`<root-directory>/playground/config/logging.yaml`.

To learn more about editing an Omni Gatewayâs logging configuration file, see
[Configuring External Logs for Omni Gateway in Local Mode](../../gateway/latest/flex-local-third-party-logs-config).

## Use the Rust Debugger

Visual Studio Code (VS Code) includes a Rust debugger that can run unit tests. You cannot use the VS
Code Debugger while the policy is deployed on an Omni Gateway. To create the API mocking, you must
use unit tests for the specific logic you need to debug.

To debug Rust code in VS Code, you must install the
[CodeLLDB Debugger Extension](https://marketplace.visualstudio.com/items?itemName=vadimcn.vscode-lldb)
or a different VS Code extension with the same functionality. To learn more about debugging Rust in
VS Code, see
[Rust in Visual Studio Code](https://code.visualstudio.com/docs/languages/rust#_debugging).

## See Also

- [Writing Integration Tests](policies-pdk-integration-tests)
- [PDK Troubleshooting](policies-pdk-troubleshooting)
---

# Developing Custom Policies

> For the full documentation index, see: https://docs.mulesoft.com/llms.txt

After installing the [Prerequisites](policies-pdk-prerequisites), you can develop custom policies by
using Omni Gateway Policy Development Kit (PDK).

The custom policy lifecycle consists of all the steps of developing a custom policy that occur in
PDK. The following steps do not include steps that occur outside of PDK, such as
[applying a policy](policies-pdk-apply-policies), which is the same for all Omni Gateway policies.

After you [create a new project](policies-pdk-create-project), you might iteratively complete some
steps. For example, while writing the policyâs source code, you might need to return to
[add additional parameters](policies-pdk-create-schema-definition), or you might need to recompile
the policy after debugging the source code.

To create a custom policy with PDK, follow these steps:

1.  [Create a new project](policies-pdk-create-project-overview).
2.  [Define a schema definition](policies-pdk-create-schema-definition).

    Modify the `definition/gcl.yaml` file to define metadata and configuration parameters for your
    custom policy.

3.  [Configure custom policy features in Rust](policies-pdk-configure-features).

    Use the rust code examples to implement your custom logic in the `src/lib.rs` file.

4.  [Compile your custom policy](policies-pdk-compile-policies).

    Compiling your policy creates the `WebAssembly` binary file and configuration files necessary to
    upload your policy to Exchange and deploy it to an Omni Gateway.

5.  [Test and debug your policy](policies-pdk-test-debug-overview).

    Use [pdk-unit](policies-pdk-unit) for fast unit tests,
    [the PDK debugging playground](policies-pdk-debug-local) for interactive checks with Omni
    Gateway in Local Mode in Docker, and [integration tests](policies-pdk-integration-tests) to
    guard against regressions with your services and gateway configuration.

6.  [Publish your policy on Exchange](policies-pdk-publish-policies-overview).

    Publish your policy on Exchange to distribute a development version of your policy for testing
    with your Omni Gateway configuration and to release production-ready policies. To deploy a
    policy to Omni Gateway, see step seven.

7.  Deploy your custom policy to a production environment by
    [applying it to an API instance](policies-pdk-apply-policies) deployed on Omni Gateway.
8.  Configure a new version of this API instance by returning to step two.
---

# Writing Integration Tests

> For the full documentation index, see: https://docs.mulesoft.com/llms.txt

Omni Gateway Policy Development Kit (PDK) provides an automated testing framework to detect
integration regressions in your custom policy.

The integration testing framework provided by PDK only supports Omni Gateway running in Local Mode
in a Docker container. If you plan to deploy or policy to Omni Gateway running in Connected Mode or
to a different platform, you should still first test your policy in PDK by using Omni Gateway
running in Local Mode in a Docker container.

> [!IMPORTANT] You can run Omni Gateway in Local Mode on Docker in Windows only to develop and test
> your policies. However, Omni Gateway does not support Windows in production environments.

To test your policy, complete the following tasks:

1.  Review [Before You Begin](#before-you-begin).
2.  Review [Integration Tests Directory Structure](#structure).
3.  [Register an Omni Gateway Instance in Local Mode](#register).
4.  [Configure Your Custom Policy](#configure-your-custom-policy).
5.  [Create a Test Function](#create-a-test-function).
6.  [Run an Integration Test](#run-integration-test).
7.  [Configure Services for Integration Testing](#configure-services-for-integration-testing).
8.  [Review Service Logs](#review-service-logs).

## Before You Begin

Ensure that you have:

- [Installed Docker](https://www.docker.com/)

## Integration Tests Directory Structure

By default, the `<root-directory>/tests` directory contains the following files:

tests âââ requests.rs âââ common âââ mod.rs âââ config âââ logging.yaml âââ registration.yaml

The `/tests` directory contains the integration tests. By default, the folder contains:

- `requests.rs` file: An individual test module and executable.
- `common/mod.rs` file: A test module that contains functionalities, API configurations, and policy
  configurations that must be included in every integration test module.

## Register an Omni Gateway Instance in Local Mode

To begin using the `make test` command to debug your custom policy, register an Omni Gateway in
Local Mode.

For the `make test` command to work, an Omni Gateway `registration.yaml` file must exist in the
`<root-directory>/tests/config` directory. Create this file by running the registration command in
the directory, or move a `registration.yaml` file of a previously registered Omni Gateway to the
directory.

To register Omni Gateway, see:

- [Register and Run with a Username and Password in a Docker Container](../../gateway/latest/flex-local-reg-run-up#docker)
- [Register and Run with a Connected App in a Docker Container](../../gateway/latest/flex-local-reg-run-app#docker)
- [Register and Run with a Token in a Docker Container](../../gateway/latest/flex-local-reg-run-token#docker)

> [!IMPORTANT] Run only the registration command. Do not run the Docker start command because the
> `make test` command completes this step.

Create a different registration file on each device testing the policy.

The registration file is ignored for storage in remote repositories and is listed in the
`.gitignore` file located at the root of the project directory.

## Configure Your Custom Policy

Each test function requires the configuration of the API and policies for that request.

To configure the custom policy:

1.  Print the policy ID by running the `show-policy-ref-name`:

    ```ssh
    make show-policy-ref-name
    ```

2.  Copy the result.
3.  Override `{{project-name}}` in `common/mod.rs` with your policy ID, for example:

    ```rust
    pub const POLICY_NAME: &str = "<custom-policy-id>";
    ```

4.  Configure the policyâs configuration parameters.

    To find your configuration parameters, see the `gcl.yaml` file.

## Create a Test Function

Add a new test module by placing a new test function in an existing test module, for example
`/tests/requests.rs`.

PDK test functions are `async` functions decorated with the `#[pdk_test]` attribute:

```rust
use pdk_test::pdk_test;

#[pdk_test]
async fn say_hello() {

}
```

> [!NOTE] Because the body is empty, this test function never fails. Run `make test` to ensure
> everything is working fine.

## Run an Integration Test

To run the integrations tests modules in the `/tests` folder, execute the `make test` command:

```ssh
make test
```

Run this test as you add additional features to your integration test to ensure proper
configuration.

> [!NOTE] The `make test` command compiles the policy before running the integration tests.

## Configure Services for Integration Testing

PDK integration tests contain a composite of services running inside a Docker network. You can
register service instances in a `TestComposite` object. Every service has a configuration, hostname,
and instance.

The `pdk-test` crate contains all the functionality for writing integration tests for custom
policies in PDK and is included in the `[dev-dependencies]` section of the `Cargo.toml` file.

### Supported Services

PDKâs test library supports the following services:

<table><colgroup><col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th>Service</th><th>Description</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p><code>Flex</code></p></td><td><p>Omni Gateway service instance.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><code>httpmock</code></p></td><td><p>Service binding to the Rustâs <a href="https://crates.io/crates/httpmock">httpmock</a> server.</p></td></tr></tbody></table>

The supported services cover the majority of use cases for integration testing. It is not possible
to define different types of service.

### Configure an httpmock Service

`httpmock` is a mock server that enables you to write request mocks in Rust. Configure an
[httpmock](https://crates.io/crates/httpmock) service by using an `HttpMockConfig` value. It is a
best practice to return a `Result` from the test function. The
[anyhow](https://crates.io/crates/anyhow) Rust crate enables you to return different default error
types.

To configure an `httpmock` service, see the following code example:

```rust
use pdk_test::{pdk_test, TestComposite};
use pdk_test::services::httpmock::HttpMockConfig;

#[pdk_test]
async fn say_hello() -> anyhow::Result<()> {

    // Configure HttpMock service
    let backend_config = HttpMockConfig::builder()
        .hostname("backend")
        .port(80) // Port where the service will accept requests
        .build();

    // Register HTTPBin service and start the docker network
    let composite = TestComposite::builder()
        .with_service(backend_config)
        .build()
        .await?;

    Ok(())
}
```

> [!NOTE] If the Docker engine is properly configured, this test passes. Run `make test` to ensure
> proper configuration.

### Configure an Alternative httpmock Docker Image

To use an `httpmock` Docker image from another source, instead of the provided default, the
configuration builder provides an `image_name()` method to define it explicitly.

To configure an alternative `httpmock` Docker image, update your `HttpMockConfig::builder()`
definition as in this example:

```rust
// Configure HttpMock service
let backend_config = HttpMockConfig::builder()
    .image_name("myrepo/httpmock") // Custom image name setting
    .hostname("backend")
    .port(80) // Port where the service will accept requests
    .build();
```

### Get a Serviceâs Handle

Every configured service instance in the composite has a handle for custom interaction. The handle
returns the sockets where the service endpoints are available.

To find a serviceâs handle, see the following code example:

```rust
use pdk_test::{pdk_test, TestComposite};
use pdk_test::services::httpmock::{HttpMock, HttpMockConfig};

#[pdk_test]
async fn say_hello() -> anyhow::Result<()> {

    // Configure HTTPMock service
    let backend_config = HttpMockConfig::builder()
        .hostname("backend")
        .build();

    // Register HTTPMock service and start the docker network
    let composite = TestComposite::builder()
        .with_service(backend_config)
        .port(80) // Port where the service accepts requests
        .build()
        .await?;

    // Get the httpmock handle
    let httpmock: HttpMock = composite.service()?;

    Ok(())
}
```

> [!NOTE] If the `httpmock` instance is properly configured, this test passes. Run `make test` to
> ensure proper configuration.

### HTTP Endpoint Requests

To mock endpoints, use the
[httpmock When/Then API](https://docs.rs/httpmock/latest/httpmock/#whenthen-api).

After mocking the endpoint, use an HTTP client to make endpoint requests.

The [reqwest](https://crates.io/crates/reqwest/) crate offers the most comprehensive and flexible
Rust HTTP client.

To mock endpoints and make requests, see the following code example:

```rust
use pdk_test::{pdk_test, TestComposite};
use pdk_test::services::httpmock::{HttpMock, HttpMockConfig};

#[pdk_test]
async fn say_hello() -> anyhow::Result<()> {

    // Configure HttpMock service
    let backend_config = HttpMockConfig::builder()
        .hostname("backend")
        .port(80) // Port where the service will accept requests
        .build();

    // Register HTTPBin service and start the docker network
    let composite = TestComposite::builder()
        .with_service(backend_config)
        .build()
        .await?;

    // Get the httpmock handle
    let httpmock: HttpMock = composite.service()?;

    // Connect the mock server
    let mock_server = httpmock::MockServer::connect_async(httpmock.socket()).await;

    // Configure the endpoint mocks
    mock_server.mock_async(|when, then| {
        when.path_contains("/hello");
        then.status(202).body("World!");
    }).await;

    let base_url = mock_server.base_url();

    // Hit the endpoint
    let response = reqwest::get(format!("{base_url}/hello")).await?;

    // Assert on response
    assert_eq!(response.status(), 202);
    assert_eq!(response.text().await?, "World!");

    Ok(())
}
```

> [!NOTE] Run `make test` to ensure proper configuration.

### Configure an Omni Service Instance

Configure a `Flex` service by registering an instance of the `FlexConfig` struct in `TestComposite`.
To complete the `FlexConfig` instance, you must also define a `ApiConfig` and `PolicyConfig`
instance.

The `FlexConfig` struct has the following properties:

<table><colgroup><col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th>Property</th><th>Content</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p><code>version</code></p></td><td><p>Omni Gateway version to test.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><code>image_name</code></p></td><td><p>Omni Gateway Docker image name, by default, "mulesoft/flex-gateway".</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><code>hostname</code></p></td><td><p>Hostname of the <code>Flex</code> service, by default, "local-flex".</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><code>config_mounts</code></p></td><td><p>A map of local directories where the <code>Flex</code> service finds configuration files. The <code>POLICY_DIR</code> and <code>COMMON_CONFIG_DIR</code> constants are defined in the <code>common/mod.rs</code> module by default. If your test requires further configuration, add a directory with the specific configuration for the test functions.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><code>with_api</code></p></td><td><p>The <code>ApiConfiguration</code> of the API deployed to the <code>Flex</code> service instance. Call multiple times to configure multiple APIs.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><code>ports</code></p></td><td><p>List of ports where the <code>Flex</code> service listens for requests. If any of your configuration directories included in <code>config_mount</code> directories expose an API, the port must match the ports configured in the <code>spec.address</code> property.</p></td></tr></tbody></table>

The `ApiConfig` has the following properties:

<table><colgroup><col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th>Property</th><th>Content</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p><code>name</code></p></td><td><p>Name of the API.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><code>upstream</code></p></td><td><p>The reference to the upstream service.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><code>path</code></p></td><td><p>Path to which the upstream requests are sent.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><code>port</code></p></td><td><p>Ports where the API listens for requests.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><code>policies</code></p></td><td><p>A List of <code>PolicyConfig</code> instances applied to the API.</p></td></tr></tbody></table>

The `PolicyConfig` has the following properties:

<table><colgroup><col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th>Property</th><th>Content</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p><code>name</code></p></td><td><p>Policy to apply.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><code>configuration</code></p></td><td><p>Parameters for the policy.</p></td></tr></tbody></table>

The following code example defines a complete `Flex` service:

```rust
mod common;
use common::*;

use pdk_test::{pdk_test, TestComposite};
use pdk_test::port::Port;
use pdk_test::services::flex::FlexConfig;

// Port where Flex listens for requests
const FLEX_PORT: Port = 8081;

#[pdk_test]
async fn say_hello() -> anyhow::Result<()> {

    // Configure an upstream service for the API
    let httpmock_config = HttpMockConfig::builder()
        .port(80)
        .version("latest")
        .hostname("backend")
        .build();

    // Configure a policy for the API
    let policy_config = PolicyConfig::builder()
        .name(POLICY_NAME)
        .configuration(serde_json::json!({"source": "http://backend/blocked", "frequency": 60}))
        .build();

    // Configure the API to deploy to the Flex
    let api_config = ApiConfig::builder()
        .name("ingress-http")
        .upstream(&httpmock_config)
        .path("/anything/echo/")
        .port(FLEX_PORT)
        .policies([policy_config])
        .build();

    // Configure the Flex service
    let flex_config = FlexConfig::builder()
        .version("1.6.1")
        .hostname("local-flex")
        .config_mounts([
            (POLICY_DIR, "policy"),
            (COMMON_CONFIG_DIR, "common")
        ])
        .with_api(api_config)
        .build();

    let composite = TestComposite::builder()
        .with_service(flex_config)
        .with_service(httpmock_config)
        .build()
        .await?;
}
```

### Configure an Alternative Omni Docker Image

To use a `flex` Docker image from another source, instead of the provided default, the configuration
builder provides an `image_name()` method to define it explicitly.

To configure an alternative Omni Docker image, update your `FlexConfig::builder()` definition as in
this example:

```rust
// Configure the Flex service
let flex_config = FlexConfig::builder()
    .image_name("myrepo/flex-gateway") // Custom image name setting
    .version("1.6.1")
    .hostname("local-flex")
    .config_mounts([
        (POLICY_DIR, "policy"),
        (COMMON_CONFIG_DIR, "common")
    ])
    .with_api(api_config)
    .build();
```

> [!NOTE] Run `make test` to ensure proper configuration.

### Omni Service Endpoint Requests

`Flex` service exposes endpoints with external base URLs dependent on the port. To access those
URLs, the Omni service instance handle provides the `external_url()` method indexed by port, for
example:

```rust
use pdk_test::{pdk_test, TestComposite, port::Port};
use pdk_test::services::flex::Flex;

const FLEX_PORT: Port = 8081;

async fn build_composite() -> anyhow::Result<TestComposite> {
    todo!("Configuration stuff comes here")
}

#[pdk_test]
async fn say_hello() -> anyhow::Result<()> {
    // Invoke a helper composite builder function for simplicity
    let composite = build_composite().await?;

    // Get the Flex service handle
    let flex: Flex = composite.service()?;

    // Get the URL for our configured port
    let flex_url = flex.external_url(FLEX_PORT).unwrap();

    let response = reqwest::get(format!("{flex_url}/hello")).await?;

    // Make assertions

    Ok(())
}
```

### Combine Multiple Services

Usually a PDK integration test includes a `Flex` service and at least one backend service.

The following code is the default test generated when the custom policy is created:

```rust
mod common;

use httpmock::MockServer;
use pdk_test::{pdk_test, TestComposite};
use pdk_test::port::Port;
use pdk_test::services::flex::{FlexConfig, Flex};
use pdk_test::services::httpmock::{HttpMockConfig, HttpMock};

use common::*;

// Directory with the configurations for the `hello` test.
const HELLO_CONFIG_DIR: &str =  concat!(env!("CARGO_MANIFEST_DIR"), "/tests/requests/hello");

// Flex port for the internal test network
const FLEX_PORT: Port = 8081;

// This integration test shows how to build a test to compose a local-flex instance
// with a MockServer backend
#[pdk_test]
async fn hello() -> anyhow::Result<()> {
    // Configure an HttpMock service
    let httpmock_config = HttpMockConfig::builder()
        .port(80)
        .version("latest")
        .hostname("backend")
        .build();

    // Configure a policy for the API
    let policy_config = PolicyConfig::builder()
        .name(POLICY_NAME)
        .configuration(serde_json::json!({"source": "http://backend/blocked", "frequency": 60}))
        .build();

    // Configure the API to deploy to the Flex
    let api_config = ApiConfig::builder()
        .name("ingress-http")
        .upstream(&httpmock_config)
        .path("/anything/echo/")
        .port(FLEX_PORT)
        .policies([policy_config])
        .build();

    // Configure the Flex service
    let flex_config = FlexConfig::builder()
        .version("1.6.1")
        .hostname("local-flex")
        .config_mounts([
            (POLICY_DIR, "policy"),
            (COMMON_CONFIG_DIR, "common")
        ])
        .with_api(api_config)
        .build();

    // Compose the services
    let composite = TestComposite::builder()
        .with_service(flex_config)
        .with_service(httpmock_config)
        .build()
        .await?;

    // Get a handle to the Flex service
    let flex: Flex = composite.service()?;

    // Get an external URL to point the Flex service
    let flex_url = flex.external_url(FLEX_PORT).unwrap();

    // Get a handle to the HttpMock service
    let httpmock: HttpMock = composite.service()?;

    // Create a MockServer
    let mock_server = MockServer::connect_async(httpmock.socket()).await;

    // Mock a /hello request
    mock_server.mock_async(|when, then| {
        when.path_contains("/hello");
        then.status(202).body("World!");
    }).await;

    // Perform an actual request
    let response = reqwest::get(format!("{flex_url}/hello")).await?;

    // Assert on the response
    assert_eq!(response.status(), 202);

    Ok(())
}
```

## Review Service Logs

After an integration test fails, you can view the service logs to find errors. To improve the
debugging experience, there is a dedicated service log in each test folder at
`<root-directory>/target/pdk-test/<module-name>/<test>/<service>.log`.

For the example, the `say_hello` test has the following service log:

```none
<root-directory>
âââ Cargo.lock
âââ Cargo.toml
âââ playground
âââ src
âââ tests
âââ target
    âââ pdk-test
        âââ requests
            âââ say_hello
                âââ <service>.log
```

## See Also

- [Debugging Custom Policies with the PDK Debugging Playground](policies-pdk-debug-local)
- [PDK Troubleshooting](policies-pdk-troubleshooting)
---

# Creating Mule Gateway PDK Policies

> For the full documentation index, see: https://docs.mulesoft.com/llms.txt

Omni Gateway Policy Development Kit (PDK) supports creating Mule Gateway policies to enhance your
experience:

- Creating policies
- Defining the configuration schema
- Deploying policies to Exchange

The PDK Anypoint CLI plugin generates custom policy folders with all required policy files. In the
folder, PDK includes a Makefile with commands to build the policy UI and deploy to Exchange.

To create Mule custom policies:

1.  Ensure you meet the [Before You Begin](#before-you-begin) prerequisites.
2.  [Create a custom policy](#create-policy).
3.  [Define a policy schema](#define-schema).
4.  [Define policy logic](#define-policy-logic).
5.  [Publish and release the policy](#publish-policies).

## Before You Begin

Before you begin, ensure you meet the these prerequisites:

- PDK supported operating system with Make installed

  See [Supported Operating Systems](policies-pdk-prerequisites#supported-operating-systems).

- Anypoint CLI

  See [Anypoint CLI PDK Requirements](policies-pdk-prerequisites#cli).

- Java

  For supported Java versions, see [Java Support](../../general/java-support).

- [Apache Maven](https://maven.apache.org/)

  Apache Maven is a prerequisite for the [Mule Maven Plugin](../../mule-runtime/latest/mmp-concept).
  PDK Mule Policy projects include an embedded Mule Maven Plugin so downloading the plugin is not
  required. To ensure your Maven version is compatible with the latest plugin, see
  [Mule Maven Plugin Release Notes](../../release-notes/mule-maven-plugin/mule-maven-plugin-release-notes).

- MuleSoftâs Maven archetype

  Configure MuleSoftâs Maven archetype by adding the following code section to Mavenâs
  `settings.xml` file:

  ```xml
   <profiles>
     <profile>
       <id>archetype-repository</id>
       <repositories>
         <repository>
           <id>archetype</id>
           <name>Mule Repository</name>
           <url>https://repository.mulesoft.org/nexus/content/repositories/public</url>
           <releases>
             <enabled>true</enabled>
             <checksumPolicy>fail</checksumPolicy>
           </releases>
           <snapshots>
             <enabled>true</enabled>
             <checksumPolicy>warn</checksumPolicy>
           </snapshots>
         </repository>
       </repositories>
     </profile>
   </profiles>
  ```

## Create a Mule Custom Policy with PDK

To create your custom policy execute:

```cmd
pdk policy-project create --name <policy-name> --implementation-technology mule4
```

Running this command generates a custom policy folder with the policy name. The folder contains a
makefile that provides all the callable actions required during the policy development lifecycle.

## Define a Policy Schema

Use the `gcl.yaml` file inside you policy project to define the policyâs schema. PDK generates your
policyâs UI json from the parameters defined in the schema definition. This replaces the previous
need of manually writing a `config.json` schema file.

To define a schema definition, see
[Defining a Policy Schema Definition](policies-pdk-create-schema-definition). Include all parameters
in schema that you reference in you `template.xml` file.

> [!NOTE] Mule Gateway custom policies support the Dataweave format, but donât support DataWeave
> Bindings. For more information, see
> [DataWeave Expressions](policies-pdk-create-schema-definition#dataweave-expressions).

## Define the Policy Logic

Define the policy logic in the `template.xml` file.

The `template.xml` file, by default, contains the logic for a basic policy that sets the returned
HTTP Response payload to a âHello World!â message. View the file to see the logic.

For more policy logic examples, see:

- [Developing a Custom Response Policy](../../mule-gateway/policies-custom-response-example)
- [Developing a Custom Policy Using the Event Authentication Extension](../../mule-gateway/policies-custom-set-authentication-example)

## Publish and Release Policies

PDK publishes Mule Gateway and Omni Gateway policies in the same way.

To publish and release policies, see
[Publishing Unified-Model Policies to Exchange](policies-pdk-publish-policies).

## See Also

- [Mule Gateway Custom Policies](../../mule-gateway/policies-custom-overview)
---

# Omni Gateway Policy Development Kit (PDK) Overview

> For the full documentation index, see: https://docs.mulesoft.com/llms.txt

Policies enforce regulations to help manage security, control traffic, and improve API adoption.
Omni Gateway provides many [included policies](../../gateway/latest/policies-included-directory),
but they might not cover all your specific business needs.

Omni Gateway (formally Flex Gateway) Policy Development Kit (PDK) is a software development kit that
enables you to develop custom policies with ease.

PDK provides instructions to guide you through the policy development lifecycle, from implementing
your Rust source code to releasing your policy on Exchange. Like included policies, you can apply
custom policies to Omni Gateway running in Connected Mode by using API Manager or to Omni Gateway
running in Local Mode by using YAML configuration files.

PDK provides [example policies](policies-pdk-policy-templates) to help you get started with policy
development and the
[MuleSoft MCP Server Policy Management Tools](../../mulesoft-mcp-server/reference-mcp-tools#policy-management)
to help manage the custom policy lifecycle.

Watch the PDK Product Spotlight video to see a quick overview of PDK.

![](https://play.vidyard.com/ibt7HZ8SByp9VCn88dNPx7.jpg)

## See Also

- [Review the architecture](policies-pdk-architecture)
- [Review the prerequisites](policies-pdk-prerequisites)
---

# Custom Policy Examples

> For the full documentation index, see: https://docs.mulesoft.com/llms.txt

Omni Gateway Policy Development Kit (PDK) provides example policy projects to help you start
configuring your Rust source code.

You can find all example policies and their documentation in the
[PDK Custom Policy GitHub Repository](https://github.com/mulesoft/pdk-custom-policy-examples).

## API Policy Examples

PDK includes the following API example policies:

- [Block Policy Example](https://github.com/mulesoft/pdk-custom-policy-examples/blob/1.8.0/block/README.md):
  Use the Block Policy as an example of how to block IP ranges by implementing
  [periodic functions](policies-pdk-configure-features-timer) and
  [HTTP calls](policies-pdk-configure-features-http-request).
- [Certification Policy Example](https://github.com/mulesoft/pdk-custom-policy-examples/blob/1.8.0/certs/README.md):
  Use the Certification Policy as an example of how to obtain data concerning connection
  certificates.
- [CORS Validation Policy Example](https://github.com/mulesoft/pdk-custom-policy-examples/blob/1.8.0/cors-validation/README.md):
  Use the CORS Validation Policy as an example of how to use functions of the
  [CORS Library](policies-pdk-configure-features-cors) in your custom policy.
- [Crypto Policy Example](https://github.com/mulesoft/pdk-custom-policy-examples/blob/1.8.0/crypto/README.md):
  Use the Crypto Policy as an example of how to use
  [third-party libraries](policies-pdk-configure-features-libraries) that provide cryptography
  capabilities in your custom policy.
- [Data Caching Policy Example](https://github.com/mulesoft/pdk-custom-policy-examples/blob/1.8.0/data-caching/README.md):
  Use the Data Caching Policy as an example of how to implement
  [data caching](policies-pdk-configure-features-caching) in your custom policy.
- [Data Storage Stats Policy Example](https://github.com/mulesoft/pdk-custom-policy-examples/blob/1.8.0/data-storage-stats/README.md):
  Use the Data Storage Stats Policy as an example of how to implement
  [data storage](policies-pdk-configure-features-data-storage) with both local (in-memory) and
  shared (Redis) data storage.
- [DataWeave Policy Example](https://github.com/mulesoft/pdk-custom-policy-examples/blob/1.8.0/dataweave/README.md):
  Use the DataWeave Policy as an example of how to handle
  [custom DataWeave expressions](policies-pdk-configure-features-dataweave) in your custom policy.
- [IP Filter Policy Example](https://github.com/mulesoft/pdk-custom-policy-examples/blob/1.8.0/ip-filter/README.md):
  Use the IP Filter Policy as an example of how to extract client IPs from request headers and
  implement IP-based access control using PDKâs
  [IP Filter feature](policies-pdk-configure-features-ip-filter).
- [JSON Validation Policy Example](https://github.com/mulesoft/pdk-custom-policy-examples/blob/1.8.0/json-validation/README.md):
  Use the JSON Validation Policy as an example of how to enforce JSON payload limits with the
  [JSON Validator library](policies-pdk-configure-features-json-validator).
- [JWT Generation Policy Example](https://github.com/mulesoft/pdk-custom-policy-examples/blob/1.8.0/jwt-generation/README.md):
  Use the JWT Generation Policy as an example of how to generate JWT tokens using the
  [JWT generation function](policies-pdk-configure-features-jwt#create-jwt-token).
- [JWT Validation Policy Example](https://github.com/mulesoft/pdk-custom-policy-examples/blob/1.8.0/jwt-validation/README.md):
  Use the JWT Validation Policy as an example of how to use functions of the
  [JWT Library](policies-pdk-configure-features-jwt) in your custom policy.
- [Metrics Policy Example](https://github.com/mulesoft/pdk-custom-policy-examples/blob/1.8.0/metrics/README.md):
  Use the Metrics Policy as an example of how to periodically send data to a metric ingestion
  service by implementing [periodic functions](policies-pdk-configure-features-timer) and
  [HTTP calls](policies-pdk-configure-features-http-request).
- [OAuth 2.0 Token Introspection Policy Example](https://github.com/mulesoft/pdk-custom-policy-examples/blob/1.8.0/oauth-2-token-introspection/README.md):
  Use the OAuth 2.0 Token Introspection Policy as an example of how to use
  [Token Introspection](policies-pdk-configure-features-token-introspection) in your custom policy.
- [Query Policy Example](https://github.com/mulesoft/pdk-custom-policy-examples/blob/1.8.0/query/README.md):
  Use the Query Policy as an example of how to handle query parameters in your custom policy.
- [Simple OAuth 2.0 Validation Policy Example](https://github.com/mulesoft/pdk-custom-policy-examples/blob/1.8.0/simple-oauth-2-validation/README.md):
  Use the Simple OAuth 2.0 Validation Policy as an example of how to implement
  [HTTP calls](policies-pdk-configure-features-http-request) in your custom policy.
- [Spike Policy Example](https://github.com/mulesoft/pdk-custom-policy-examples/blob/1.8.0/spike/README.md):
  Use the Spike Policy as an example of how to implement spike arrest with the
  [Spike Control library](policies-pdk-configure-features-spike-control) and periodic ticking with
  [Timer functions](policies-pdk-configure-features-timer).
- [Stream Payload Policy Example](https://github.com/mulesoft/pdk-custom-policy-examples/blob/1.8.0/stream-payload/README.md):
  Use the Stream Payload Policy as an example of how to
  [read payload bodies larger than 1MB](policies-pdk-configure-features-headers#streaming-bodies).
- [TLS Calls Policy Example](https://github.com/mulesoft/pdk-custom-policy-examples/blob/1.8.0/tls-calls/README.md):
  Use the TLS Calls Policy as an example of how to make HTTPS calls in your policy without
  installing certificates on the operating system running Omni Gateway.
- [XML Validation Policy Example](https://github.com/mulesoft/pdk-custom-policy-examples/blob/1.8.0/xml-validation/README.md):
  Use the XML Validation Policy as an example of how to enforce XML payload limits with the
  [XML Validator library](policies-pdk-configure-features-xml-validator).

## A2A and MCP Server Policy Examples

PDK includes the following A2A and MCP example policies:

- [A2A PII Detector Policy Example](https://github.com/mulesoft/pdk-custom-policy-examples/blob/main/agent-policies/a2a-pii-detector-policy/README.md):
  Demonstrates how to log or block sensitive information in A2A agent interactions.
- [A2A Prompt Decorator Policy Example](https://github.com/mulesoft/pdk-custom-policy-examples/blob/main/agent-policies/a2a-prompt-decorator-policy/README.md):
  Shows how to modify prompts sent to an A2A agent.
- [A2A Support Policy Example](https://github.com/mulesoft/pdk-custom-policy-examples/blob/main/agent-policies/a2a-support-policy/README.md):
  Demonstrates how to add A2A support.
- [MCP Support Policy Example](https://github.com/mulesoft/pdk-custom-policy-examples/blob/main/agent-policies/mcp-support-policy/README.md):
  Shows how to add MCP support.

## Set Up an Example Policy Project

To use an example policy project:

1.  Make sure you have installed all [PDK prerequisites](policies-pdk-prerequisites).
2.  Clone the
    [PDK Custom Policy GitHub Repository](https://github.com/mulesoft/pdk-custom-policy-examples)
    using the following command or your preferred GitHub interface:

    ```ssh
    git clone https://github.com/mulesoft/pdk-custom-policy-examples.git
    ```

3.  Navigate to the newly cloned directory, then copy and paste the directory of your desired policy
    into a new location.
4.  In the `cargo.toml` file of the copied policy example, replace the `group-id` with your group
    ID, for example:

    ```toml
    group_id = "<your-group-id>"
    ```

5.  Run the `make setup` command:

    ```ssh
    make setup
    ```

6.  Navigate to the `README.md` file in the example policy to read more about the policy and how to
    test it.

## See Also

- [Implementing Your Custom Policy Features in Rust](policies-pdk-configure-features)
---

# Prerequisites for Omni Gateway Policy Development Kit (PDK)

> For the full documentation index, see: https://docs.mulesoft.com/llms.txt

Before you begin using Omni Gateway Policy Development Kit (PDK), review these prerequisites.

## Omni Gateway

PDK tutorials assume that you have [downloaded Omni Gateway](../../gateway/latest/flex-install) and
have some prior knowledge of the product.

## Rust Requirements for Using PDK

1.  Git

    To download and install, see
    [Installing Git](https://git-scm.com/book/en/v2/Getting-Started-Installing-Git).

2.  Rust programming language version v1.88.0 or later

    To install or update Rust, run the following curl command:

    ```ssh
    curl --proto '=https' --tlsv1.2 -sSf https://sh.rustup.rs | sh
    ```

    > [!NOTE] If you do not have curl installed, see [Install curl](https://curl.se/download.html).

3.  Rust WebAssembly System Interface (WASI) Crate

    To install or update Rust WASI, run the following command:

    ```ssh
    rustup target add wasm32-wasip1
    ```

4.  Cargo Generate

    To install or update Cargo Generate, run the following command:

    ```ssh
    cargo install --locked cargo-generate@0.22.0
    ```

## Supported Operating Systems

- macOS
- Ubuntu

  To use Ubuntu, execute the following commands to install the required packages after installing
  the [Rust Requirements for Using PDK](#rust-requirements):

  ```ssh
  sudo apt install -y build-essential
  sudo apt install -y pkg-config
  sudo apt install -y libssl-dev
  ```

- Windows

  > [!IMPORTANT] PDK supports Windows to develop and test you custom policy. However, Omni Gateway
  > does not support Windows in production environments.

  To execute the `make` commands included in PDK, install:
  1.  [PowerShell on Windows](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/powershell/scripting/install/installing-powershell-on-windows?view=powershell-7.3)
  2.  Make, with either:
      - [Make for Windows](https://gnuwin32.sourceforge.net/packages/make.htm)
      - Chocolatey:

        ```ssh
        choco install make
        ```

## Anypoint CLI

To configure the Anypoint CLI for PDK:

1.  [Install Anypoint CLI 4.x v1.4.4 or later](../../anypoint-cli/latest/install).

    > [!NOTE] To find your Anypoint CLI 4.x version, see
    > [Verify the Core Package Version](../../anypoint-cli/latest/install#verify-installation).

2.  [Authenticate to the Anypoint Platform CLI](../../anypoint-cli/latest/auth) using a
    [Connected App](../../access-management/connected-apps-overview).

    Authenticate with a Connected App by providing the appâs client ID and client secret.

    > [!NOTE] In addition to the `View Organization` and `View Environment` scopes required to
    > authenticate to Anypoint Platform, to complete
    > [Publishing Unified-Model Policies to Exchange](policies-pdk-publish-policies), the Connected
    > App must have the `Exchange Contributor` scope applied.

3.  Run the following command to install or update the Anypoint CLI PDK Plugin:

    ```ssh
    anypoint-cli-v4 plugins:install anypoint-pdk-plugin
    ```

## Docker

Docker is required to use the [local debugging environment](policies-pdk-debug-local) and the
[integration testing framework](policies-pdk-integration-tests) provided with PDK. You can still
build and publish policies without docker.

To install Docker, see [Install Docker](https://docs.docker.com/get-docker/).

> [!IMPORTANT] You can run Omni Gateway in Local Mode on Docker in Windows only to develop and test
> your policies. However, Omni Gateway does not support Windows in production environments.

## Visual Studio (Suggested IDE)

You can use PDK in any IDE or text editor, such as
[Microsoft Visual Studio](https://code.visualstudio.com/) with the
[Rust in Visual Studio Code Plugin](https://code.visualstudio.com/docs/languages/rust) installed.

## See Also

- [Upgrading PDK](policies-pdk-upgrade-pdk)
- [Policy Development Kit Architecture Overview](policies-pdk-architecture)
- [Developing Custom Policies](policies-pdk-develop-custom-policies)
---

# Publishing Custom Policies to Exchange

> For the full documentation index, see: https://docs.mulesoft.com/llms.txt

During the custom policy development cycle, you can upload your policy to Anypoint Exchange to
create and manage policy assets, control versions, and distribute policies to other users or
gateways. For more information about Exchange, see [Anypoint Exchange Overview](../../exchange/).

The steps and commands differ depending on whether your policy is a unified-model or split-model
project. Choose the guide that matches your project:

- [Uploading a Unified-Model Policy to Exchange](policies-pdk-publish-policies)
- [Uploading a Split-Model Policy to Exchange](policies-pdk-publish-policies-split)

After you upload your policy to Exchange, see
[Applying Custom Policies](policies-pdk-apply-policies) to apply the policy to your APIs.

## See Also

- [Applying Custom Policies](policies-pdk-apply-policies)
- [PDK Troubleshooting](policies-pdk-troubleshooting)
---

# Publishing Split-Model Policies to Exchange

> For the full documentation index, see: https://docs.mulesoft.com/llms.txt

During the custom split-model policy development cycle, you can upload the definition and
implementation of your policy to Exchange to create a new Exchange assets to manage the asset
versions and to distribute it to other users. For more information about Exchange, see
[Anypoint Exchange Overview](../../exchange/).

For custom policies running in Local Mode, it is not required to publish your policy to Exchange.
However, Exchange is a useful version control tool. It is your responsibility to distribute custom
policies for Local Mode use.

When uploading your policy to Exchange, you can choose to either:

- [Publish a Custom Policy](#publish)  
  Publish a policy if the policy version is still in development. This enables you to test and edit
  the policy version.

  To learn about development assets, see
  [Publish a Custom Policy](policies-pdk-publish-policies#publish).

- [Release a Custom Policy](#release)  
  Release the policy to create a definitive version of the policy. After releasing the policy, you
  must create a new policy version to edit the policy.

  To learn about production assets, see
  [Release a Custom Policy](policies-pdk-publish-policies#release).

Every time you publish or release a definition and implementation asset, you create a new `stable`
version of that asset in Exchange. For more information about `stable` state assets, see
[Stable State Assets](../../exchange/lifecycle#stablet-state).

After you upload your definition and implementation assets to Exchange, see
[Applying Custom Policies](policies-pdk-apply-policies).

## Before You Begin

- Ensure that the Connected App you used to authenticate to the
  [Anypoint Platform CLI](policies-pdk-prerequisites#cli) has the `Exchange Contributor` scope
  applied.
- [Compile your Policy](policies-pdk-compile-policies).

## Publish a New Split-Model Policy

1.  [Create a definition project](policies-pdk-create-project-split#create-a-new-policy-definition-project):
    1.  Make changes on the `gcl.yaml`
    2.  Execute `make publish` in the definition project to publish the development definition asset
        to Exchange.

2.  [Create an implementation project](policies-pdk-create-project-split#create-a-new-policy-implementation-project):
    1.  Update the `definition_asset_id` version field in the `cargo.toml` file to `1.0.0-DEV`.
    2.  Execute `make build-asset-files` in the implementation project.
    3.  Code your policy logic and test locally.
    4.  Execute `make publish` to release the development implementation asset to Exchange for
        testing in lower environments.
    5.  Validate the policy works as expected. Complete steps C and D until testing is complete.

3.  After validating the policy works as expected, release the both assets to Exchange:
    1.  In the definition project, execute `make release` to release the definition asset to
        Exchange.
    2.  In the implementation project, update the `definition_asset_id` version field in the
        `cargo.toml` file to `1.0.0`.
    3.  Execute `make release` to release the implementation asset to Exchange.

## Publish a Patch Version Update (Implementation Update)

After you have [Published a new split-model policy](#publish-a-new-split-model-policy), you can
update the implementation asset for bug fixes or performance updates:

1.  Develop the new policy implementation:
    1.  In the implementation project, update the patch number of version in the `cargo.toml` file.
        For example: `1.0.1`.
    2.  Add the `-DEV` suffix to the `definition_asset_id` version field in the `cargo.toml`. For
        example: `1.0.0-DEV`.
    3.  Execute `make build-asset-files` in the implementation project.
    4.  Code your policy logic and test locally.
    5.  Execute `make publish` to release the development implementation asset to Exchange for
        testing in lower environments.
    6.  Validate the policy works as expected. Complete steps D and E until testing is complete.

2.  After validating the policy works as expected, release the implementation asset to Exchange:
    1.  In the implementation project, remove the `-DEV` suffix from the `definition_asset_id`
        version field in the `cargo.toml` file. For example: `1.0.0-DEV` to `1.0.0`.
    2.  Execute `make release` to release the implementation asset to Exchange.

## Publish a Minor or Major Version Update (Definition and Implementation Update)

After you have [Published a new split-model policy](#publish-a-new-split-model-policy), you can
update the definition and implementation assets for updates that require new parameters:

1.  Update the definition asset:
    1.  Update the version in the `exchange.json` file of your definition project to `1.1.0`.
    2.  Make changes to the `gcl.yaml` file.
    3.  `make publish` on the definition project to publish the development definition asset to
        Exchange.

2.  Update the implementation asset:
    1.  In the implementation project, update the minor or major number of version in the
        `cargo.toml` file. For example: `1.0.0` to `1.1.0`.
    2.  Update and add the `-DEV` suffix to the `definition_asset_id` version field in the
        `cargo.toml`. For example: `1.0.0-DEV` to `1.1.0-DEV`.
    3.  Execute `make build-asset-files` in the implementation project.
    4.  Code your policy logic and test locally.
    5.  Execute `make publish` to release the development implementation asset to Exchange for
        testing in lower environments.
    6.  Validate the policy works as expected. Complete steps D and E until testing is complete.

3.  After validating the policy works as expected, release both assets to Exchange:
    1.  In the implementation project, remove the `-DEV` suffix from the `definition_asset_id`
        version field in the `cargo.toml` file. For example: `1.1.0-DEV` to `1.1.0`.
    2.  Execute `make release` to release the implementation asset to Exchange.

## Develop Split Model Policies Locally Without Publishing to Exchange

1.  Develop the policy definition:
    1.  Ensure the version in the `exchange.json` file is the correct version. For example: `1.0.0`
        for your first version.
    2.  Make changes to the `gcl.yaml` file.
    3.  Execute `make release-local` in the definition project.

2.  Develop the policy implementation:
    1.  Add the `-DEV` suffix to the `definition_asset_id` version field in the `cargo.toml` and
        ensure it matches the definition asset version used in step one.
    2.  Execute `make build-asset-files` in the implementation project.
    3.  Code your policy logic and test locally.
    4.  Validate the policy works as expected. Complete steps C until testing is complete.

3.  If you wish to distribute the policy locally as a production asset:
    1.  Remove the `-DEV` suffix from the `definition_asset_id` version field in the `cargo.toml`
        file. For example: `1.0.0-DEV` to `1.0.0`.
    2.  Execute `make build` to compile the implementation asset.
    3.  Distribute the policy assets as desired.

## See Also

- [Applying Custom Policies](policies-pdk-apply-policies)
- [PDK Troubleshooting](policies-pdk-troubleshooting)
---

# Publishing Unified-Model Policies to Exchange

> For the full documentation index, see: https://docs.mulesoft.com/llms.txt

During the custom policy development cycle, you can upload your policy to Exchange to create a new
Exchange asset to manage the policyâs version and to distribute it to other users. For more
information about Exchange, see [Anypoint Exchange Overview](../../exchange/).

For custom policies running in Local Mode, it is not required to publish your policy to Exchange.
However, Exchange is a useful version control tool. It is your responsibility to distribute custom
policies for Local Mode use.

When uploading your policy to Exchange, you can choose to either:

- [Publish a Custom Policy](#publish)  
  Publish a policy if the policy version is still in development. This enables you to test and edit
  the policy version.
- [Release a Custom Policy](#release)  
  Release the policy to create a definitive version of the policy. After releasing the policy, you
  must create a new policy version to edit the policy.

Every time you publish or release a policy, you create a new `stable` asset in Exchange. For more
information about `stable` state assets, see
[Stable State Assets](../../exchange/lifecycle#stablet-state).

After you upload your policy to Exchange, see
[Applying Custom Policies](policies-pdk-apply-policies).

## Before You Begin

- Ensure that the Connected App you used to authenticate to the
  [Anypoint Platform CLI](policies-pdk-prerequisites#cli) has the `Exchange Contributor` scope
  applied.
- [Compile your Policy](policies-pdk-compile-policies).

## Publish a Custom Policy

To publish a custom policy, run the `make publish` command from the policyâs root folder:

```ssh
make publish
```

After publishing the asset, a new asset labeled `DEV` appears in Exchange:

![Policy appears in Exchange labeled DEV.](_images/pdk-dev-asset.png)

The assetâs group ID, asset ID, and asset version (GAV) appear, similar to the following:

- **groupID**: `<organizationId>`
- **assetID**: `my-custom-policy-dev`

  When publishing a policy, `-dev` appears appended to the **assetID**.

- **version**: `1.0.0-20230618115723`

  When publishing a policy, a timestamp appears appended to the **version**.

Rerunning the publish command creates a new version of the policy asset with an updated timestamp.
Previously published policy versions are still available.

## Release a Custom Policy

To release a custom policy, run the `make release` command from the policyâs root folder:

```ssh
make release
```

Released policies are not labeled `DEV`. The assetâs GAV appears similar to the following:

- **groupID**: `<organizationId>`
- **assetID**: `my-custom-policy`

  Released policies do not have `-dev` appended to their **assetID**.

- **version**: `1.0.0`

  Released policies do not have a timestamp appended to their **assetID**.

After releasing the asset, rerunning the `make release` command results in a conflict error.

To begin working on the next version of your policy, update the version number in the `Cargo.toml`
file.

For example, if the latest version is `1.0.0`, update the `Cargo.toml` file as follows to begin
working on a minor release:

```toml
name = "my-custom-policy"
version = "1.1.0"
```

## See Also

- [Applying Custom Policies](policies-pdk-apply-policies)
- [PDK Troubleshooting](policies-pdk-troubleshooting)
---

# Omni Gateway Policy Development Kit (PDK) Release Notes

> For the full documentation index, see: https://docs.mulesoft.com/llms.txt

Omni Gateway Policy Development Kit (PDK) is a software development kit that enables you to develop
Omni Gateway custom policies with ease.

PDK provides instructions to guide you through the policy development lifecycle, from implementing
your Rust source code to releasing your policy on Exchange. Like included policies, you can apply
custom policies to Omni Gateway running in Connected Mode by using API Manager or to Omni Gateway
running in Local Mode by using YAML configuration files.

The following release notes are provided for PDK:

- [Omni Gateway Policy Development Kit (PDK) Release Notes](../../release-notes/pdk/pdk-release-notes)

## See Also

- [Omni Gateway Policy Development Kit (PDK) Overview](policies-pdk-overview)
- [Omni Gateway Overview](../../gateway/latest/)
---

# Testing and Debugging Custom Policies

> For the full documentation index, see: https://docs.mulesoft.com/llms.txt

Use the Policy Development Kit (PDK) testing options to validate behavior, catch regressions, and
debug before you [publish your policy to Exchange](policies-pdk-publish-policies-overview):

- [Debugging Custom Policies with the PDK Debugging Playground](policies-pdk-debug-local) â Use the
  preconfigured API instance in your projectâs `playground` directory to exercise and debug the
  policy interactively.
- [Writing Unit Tests](policies-pdk-unit): Run fast, deterministic Rust unit tests that exercise the
  full request and response lifecycle without configuring Envoy, WebAssembly, or external services.
- [Writing Integration Tests](policies-pdk-integration-tests): Run automated tests on a local mode
  Omni Gateway to verify your policy with real gateway behavior and surrounding configuration.
---

# PDK Troubleshooting

> For the full documentation index, see: https://docs.mulesoft.com/llms.txt

When using Omni Gateway Policy Development Kit (PDK), you might encounter issues with PDK itself.
See the following sections to help you resolve these issues:

- [Cargo-Generate Error on a Mac Device](#cargo-generate)
- [Anypoint CLI Credentials Error](#cli-credential)
- [Group ID Error When Creating the Policy Project](#group-id-error)
- [401 Authorization Error When Running Make Setup](#authorization-error)
- [Error: No such file or directory When Running Make Build (Split Model Policy)](#implementation-build-no-file)
- [Error Looking for the Specified Asset When Running Make Build-Asset-Files (Split Model Policy)](#build-asset-files-asset-not-found)

To debug issues with your custom policy, see:

- [Debugging Custom Policies with the PDK Debugging Playground](policies-pdk-debug-local)
- [Debugging Deployed Custom Policies](policies-pdk-debug-deployed-policies)

## Cargo-Generate Error on a Mac Device

When compiling Rust on a Mac Device for the first time, the following error might occur:

```ssh
$ cargo install cargo-generate
```
